--- title: "Data wrangling II" subtitle: "POL51" author: "Haley Daarstad" institute: "University of California, Davis" date: today date-format: long format: revealjs: chalkboard: true slide-number: true height: 900 width: 1600 code-line-numbers: true smooth-scrawl: true theme: [simple, html/custom.scss] incremental: true title-slide-attributes: data-background-image: img/dubois-spiral-2.png data-background-size: contain data-background-position: right data-background-color: "#dc354a" --- # Plan for today {.center background-color="#dc354a"} . . . Making amends + objects review . . . Mutating new variables . . . Creating categories # Weekly check-in {.center background-color="#dc354a"} So far, you: . . . somehow do not know how to make every graph from scratch based solely off memory . . . are confused about errors you are seeing for the first time in a computer program you've never used before . . . are unable to re-type all the code I am presenting on slides into your notes at what would be a rate on par with a professional court stenographer # Weekly check-in {.center background-color="#dc354a"} You are *confused* and *unsure* of yourselves . . . But you are doing **well** . . . You've only been coding for a single week . . . You just need to know how to piece together answers from notes + slides . . . You will **slowly** get better at coding, and dealing with errors (be patient!) # Back to objects {.center background-color="#dc354a"} ## Using objects ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: . . . What if I wanted to make this same plot, but only looking at **Asian** countries? ## Subset to Asia I can use `filter()` to subset the data to Asia, store it as a new object `gap_asia`, and use that new object to make a plot ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gap_asia = gapminder |> filter(continent == "Asia") ggplot(gap_asia, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Using the new object Notice how I need to **use the new object** the original `gapminder` does not have what I want ::::::: columns :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gap_asia, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ::: :::: :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp)) + geom_point() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ::: :::: ::::::: ## filter() and variables types Filtering requires knowing the **type** of variable you are working with . . . Numerical variables do not use quotes ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder |> filter(year > 2000) ``` ::: . . . Categorical variables use quotes, and spelling must be *exact* ✅ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder |> filter(continent == "Asia") ``` ::: . . . ❌ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder |> filter(continent == "asia") ``` ::: . . . `TRUE/FALSE` variables are all-caps, no quotes ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder |> filter(is_asia == TRUE) ``` ::: ## What values does a variable take on? To figure out what **values** a variable can take on, you can use `distinct()` . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} bot |> distinct(religion) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 13 × 1 religion 1 Roman Catholic 2 Protestant 3 Jewish 4 Nothing in particular 5 Agnostic 6 Atheist 7 Something else 8 Mormon 9 Hindu 10 Muslim 11 Eastern or Greek Orthodox 12 Buddhist 13 ``` ::: ::: # Next on data wrangling... {.center background-color="#dc354a"} So far, you: . . . You know to save/store new things into an object . . . You know how to filter the data to see only certain values for certain variables . . . What if we need a new variable? ## Homicides in 2019 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
state city population murder_total
Alabama Mobile 248431 50
Alaska Anchorage 296188 27
Arizona Chandler 249355 5
Arizona Gilbert 242090 5
Arizona Glendale 249273 12
Arizona Mesa 492268 23
````` ::: ::: ## Most violent cities in America? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Fewer deaths in towns with less people ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Normalizing variables {.center} To compare across cities we need to **take into account** differences in population . . . We want to know how many murders have taken place (or drug overdoses, or crimes, or COVID cases, or...) per person in the city (**per capita**) . . . This is called **normalizing** a variable; *changing* it so that we can make units more *comparable* ## Murder **rate** In math terms this is just dividing the number of murders by population: $Murders_{capita} = \frac{Murders}{Population}$ = "how many murders per person" . . .
Since this fraction is *tiny*, the convention is to multiply by a number that makes sense . . . for the population of a city, say 100,000 people . . . $Murders_{per 100k} = \frac{Murders}{Population} \times 100,000$ = "how many murders per 100,000 people" ## Comparing murder *rates* If we look at murder **rates**, the picture changes: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: # Making new variables with `mutate()` `mutate()` **adds** new variables to data ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](img/mutate.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ## Using mutate ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_set ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 100 × 4 state city population murder_total 1 Alabama Mobile 248431 50.0 2 Alaska Anchorage 296188 27.0 3 Arizona Chandler 249355 5.01 4 Arizona Gilbert 242090 5.01 5 Arizona Glendale 249273 12.0 6 Arizona Mesa 492268 23.0 7 Arizona Phoenix 1608139 154. 8 Arizona Scottsdale 251840 5.01 9 Arizona Tucson 532323 46.0 10 California Anaheim 353400 10.0 # ℹ 90 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Using mutate ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_set |> mutate() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 100 × 4 state city population murder_total 1 Alabama Mobile 248431 50.0 2 Alaska Anchorage 296188 27.0 3 Arizona Chandler 249355 5.01 4 Arizona Gilbert 242090 5.01 5 Arizona Glendale 249273 12.0 6 Arizona Mesa 492268 23.0 7 Arizona Phoenix 1608139 154. 8 Arizona Scottsdale 251840 5.01 9 Arizona Tucson 532323 46.0 10 California Anaheim 353400 10.0 # ℹ 90 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Using mutate ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_set |> mutate(murder_capita = murder_total / population) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 100 × 5 state city population murder_total murder_capita 1 Alabama Mobile 248431 50.0 0.000201 2 Alaska Anchorage 296188 27.0 0.0000912 3 Arizona Chandler 249355 5.01 0.0000201 4 Arizona Gilbert 242090 5.01 0.0000207 5 Arizona Glendale 249273 12.0 0.0000481 6 Arizona Mesa 492268 23.0 0.0000467 7 Arizona Phoenix 1608139 154. 0.0000955 8 Arizona Scottsdale 251840 5.01 0.0000199 9 Arizona Tucson 532323 46.0 0.0000864 10 California Anaheim 353400 10.0 0.0000283 # ℹ 90 more rows ``` ::: ::: Notice that I named a new variable, `murder_capita` ## Using mutate ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_set |> mutate(murder_capita = murder_total / population, murder_rate = murder_capita * 100000) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 100 × 6 state city population murder_total murder_capita murder_rate 1 Alabama Mobile 248431 50.0 0.000201 20.1 2 Alaska Anchorage 296188 27.0 0.0000912 9.12 3 Arizona Chandler 249355 5.01 0.0000201 2.01 4 Arizona Gilbert 242090 5.01 0.0000207 2.07 5 Arizona Glendale 249273 12.0 0.0000481 4.81 6 Arizona Mesa 492268 23.0 0.0000467 4.67 7 Arizona Phoenix 1608139 154. 0.0000955 9.55 8 Arizona Scottsdale 251840 5.01 0.0000199 1.99 9 Arizona Tucson 532323 46.0 0.0000864 8.64 10 California Anaheim 353400 10.0 0.0000283 2.83 # ℹ 90 more rows ``` ::: ::: Notice the new **columns** ## Forgetting to store If you don't store your changes, they will melt away, like tears in the rain ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_set |> mutate(murder_capita = murder_total / population, murder_rate = murder_capita * 100000) ``` ::: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_set ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 100 × 4 state city population murder_total 1 Alabama Mobile 248431 50.0 2 Alaska Anchorage 296188 27.0 3 Arizona Chandler 249355 5.01 4 Arizona Gilbert 242090 5.01 5 Arizona Glendale 249273 12.0 6 Arizona Mesa 492268 23.0 7 Arizona Phoenix 1608139 154. 8 Arizona Scottsdale 251840 5.01 9 Arizona Tucson 532323 46.0 10 California Anaheim 353400 10.0 # ℹ 90 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## New object, or overwite the old one? . . . You can make a new object: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_rates = murder_set |> mutate(murder_capita = murder_total / population, murder_rate = murder_capita * 100000) ``` ::: . . . Or overwrite the old one ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_set = murder_set |> mutate(murder_capita = murder_total / population, murder_rate = murder_capita * 100000) ``` ::: . . . In general, overwriting when you are **adding** stuff to your data (`mutate`) is fine ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} murder_set ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 100 × 6 state city population murder_total murder_capita murder_rate 1 Alabama Mobile 248431 50.0 0.000201 20.1 2 Alaska Anchorage 296188 27.0 0.0000912 9.12 3 Arizona Chandler 249355 5.01 0.0000201 2.01 4 Arizona Gilbert 242090 5.01 0.0000207 2.07 5 Arizona Glendale 249273 12.0 0.0000481 4.81 6 Arizona Mesa 492268 23.0 0.0000467 4.67 7 Arizona Phoenix 1608139 154. 0.0000955 9.55 8 Arizona Scottsdale 251840 5.01 0.0000199 1.99 9 Arizona Tucson 532323 46.0 0.0000864 8.64 10 California Anaheim 353400 10.0 0.0000283 2.83 # ℹ 90 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## New object, or overwite the old one? Overwriting when you are **removing** information from the data can be bad . . . For example, when we `filter()` . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder = gapminder |> filter(continent == "Africa") gapminder ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 624 × 6 country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap 1 Algeria Africa 1952 43.1 9279525 2449. 2 Algeria Africa 1957 45.7 10270856 3014. 3 Algeria Africa 1962 48.3 11000948 2551. 4 Algeria Africa 1967 51.4 12760499 3247. 5 Algeria Africa 1972 54.5 14760787 4183. 6 Algeria Africa 1977 58.0 17152804 4910. 7 Algeria Africa 1982 61.4 20033753 5745. 8 Algeria Africa 1987 65.8 23254956 5681. 9 Algeria Africa 1992 67.7 26298373 5023. 10 Algeria Africa 1997 69.2 29072015 4797. # ℹ 614 more rows ``` ::: ::: . . . This is bad, since we lose the original `gapminder` data ## 🚨 Your turn 🌡️ Climate change 🌡️ 🚨 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Sample from climate dataset
country year population co2
Botswana 1972 667096 0.02
Belarus 2016 9445638 59.85
Martinique 1972 327301 0.78
Tajikistan 1832 520732 0.00
Bhutan 1983 439823 0.03
````` ::: ::: ## 🚨 Your turn 🌡️ Climate change 🌡️ 🚨 Using `climate`, make the following two plots looking **only** at Germany, USA, China, India: 1. A grouped time-series of `co2` emissions over time (separate country by color) 2. A grouped time-series of `co2` emissions **per capita** over time (separate country by color) 3. Who's to "blame" for climate change? And where should we focus environmental efforts? ::: callout-tip 1. The climate data set is in the package `juanr` 2. Use `geom_line()` for the time series graph 3. Reminder to load/attach your packages using `library()` ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## 🚨 🌡️ Climate change Pt. 1 🌡️ 🚨 {.smaller} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(juanr) climate_cap <- climate |> filter(country %in% c("Germany", "United States", "China", "India")) |> mutate(co2_capita = co2/population) ggplot(climate_cap, aes(x=year, y = co2, color = country)) + geom_line() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-27-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## 🚨 🌡️ Climate change Pt. 2 🌡️ 🚨 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(juanr) ggplot(climate_cap, aes(x=year, y = co2_capita, color = country)) + geom_line() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-4_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-28-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: # Creating categories ## Creating categories with `case_when()` . . . Sometimes we want to create **categorical variables** (tall, short, poor, rich, Republican-leaning, Democrat-leaning, etc.) . . . We can use `case_when()`, which goes **within** `mutate()` . . . Like `filter()`, `case_when()` also relies on logical operators ## Who won the county? ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
sample from elections dataset
name state per_gop_2020 per_dem_2020
Guadalupe County TX 0.61 0.37
Marion County WV 0.63 0.35
Clarke County VA 0.56 0.42
Montgomery County MS 0.57 0.42
Jackson County WI 0.57 0.42
Evans County GA 0.68 0.31
Coahoma County MS 0.28 0.71
Utah County UT 0.68 0.27
Alachua County FL 0.36 0.63
Dickinson County MI 0.66 0.33
````` ::: ::: ## Who won the county? The general formula: `case_when(CONDITION ~ LABEL)` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} small_elections ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3,152 × 4 name state per_gop_2020 per_dem_2020 1 Autauga County AL 0.714 0.270 2 Baldwin County AL 0.762 0.224 3 Barbour County AL 0.535 0.458 4 Bibb County AL 0.784 0.207 5 Blount County AL 0.896 0.0957 6 Bullock County AL 0.248 0.747 7 Butler County AL 0.575 0.418 8 Calhoun County AL 0.688 0.298 9 Chambers County AL 0.573 0.416 10 Cherokee County AL 0.860 0.132 # ℹ 3,142 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Who won the county? The general formula: `case_when(CONDITION ~ LABEL)` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} small_elections |> mutate(who_won = case_when(per_gop_2020 > per_dem_2020 ~ "Republicans")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3,152 × 5 name state per_gop_2020 per_dem_2020 who_won 1 Autauga County AL 0.714 0.270 Republicans 2 Baldwin County AL 0.762 0.224 Republicans 3 Barbour County AL 0.535 0.458 Republicans 4 Bibb County AL 0.784 0.207 Republicans 5 Blount County AL 0.896 0.0957 Republicans 6 Bullock County AL 0.248 0.747 7 Butler County AL 0.575 0.418 Republicans 8 Calhoun County AL 0.688 0.298 Republicans 9 Chambers County AL 0.573 0.416 Republicans 10 Cherokee County AL 0.860 0.132 Republicans # ℹ 3,142 more rows ``` ::: ::: ::: callout-note Remember when we `mutate()` we have to name the new variable ::: ::: notes point out the NA ::: ## Who won the county? The general formula: `case_when(CONDITION ~ LABEL)` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} small_elections |> mutate(who_won = case_when(per_gop_2020 > per_dem_2020 ~ "Republicans", per_dem_2020 > per_gop_2020 ~ "Democrats")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3,152 × 5 name state per_gop_2020 per_dem_2020 who_won 1 Autauga County AL 0.714 0.270 Republicans 2 Baldwin County AL 0.762 0.224 Republicans 3 Barbour County AL 0.535 0.458 Republicans 4 Bibb County AL 0.784 0.207 Republicans 5 Blount County AL 0.896 0.0957 Republicans 6 Bullock County AL 0.248 0.747 Democrats 7 Butler County AL 0.575 0.418 Republicans 8 Calhoun County AL 0.688 0.298 Republicans 9 Chambers County AL 0.573 0.416 Republicans 10 Cherokee County AL 0.860 0.132 Republicans # ℹ 3,142 more rows ``` ::: ::: ::: notes How would I add condition for tie? ::: ## OK but by how much? Our categories can be more complex, for instance the **win margin** . . . Step 1: calculate the win **margin** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} small_elections |> mutate(margin = per_gop_2020 - per_dem_2020) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3,152 × 5 name state per_gop_2020 per_dem_2020 margin 1 Autauga County AL 0.714 0.270 0.444 2 Baldwin County AL 0.762 0.224 0.538 3 Barbour County AL 0.535 0.458 0.0766 4 Bibb County AL 0.784 0.207 0.577 5 Blount County AL 0.896 0.0957 0.800 6 Bullock County AL 0.248 0.747 -0.499 7 Butler County AL 0.575 0.418 0.157 8 Calhoun County AL 0.688 0.298 0.390 9 Chambers County AL 0.573 0.416 0.156 10 Cherokee County AL 0.860 0.132 0.728 # ℹ 3,142 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## OK but by how much? Step 2: add the categories ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} small_elections |> mutate(margin = per_gop_2020 - per_dem_2020) |> mutate(who_won = case_when(margin > 0 & margin < .1 ~ "R < 10")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3,152 × 6 name state per_gop_2020 per_dem_2020 margin who_won 1 Autauga County AL 0.714 0.270 0.444 2 Baldwin County AL 0.762 0.224 0.538 3 Barbour County AL 0.535 0.458 0.0766 R < 10 4 Bibb County AL 0.784 0.207 0.577 5 Blount County AL 0.896 0.0957 0.800 6 Bullock County AL 0.248 0.747 -0.499 7 Butler County AL 0.575 0.418 0.157 8 Calhoun County AL 0.688 0.298 0.390 9 Chambers County AL 0.573 0.416 0.156 10 Cherokee County AL 0.860 0.132 0.728 # ℹ 3,142 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## OK but by how much? Step 3: keep adding categories ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} small_elections |> mutate(margin = per_gop_2020 - per_dem_2020) |> mutate(who_won = case_when(margin > 0 & margin < .1 ~ "R < 10", margin >= .1 & margin < .2 ~ "R + 10")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3,152 × 6 name state per_gop_2020 per_dem_2020 margin who_won 1 Autauga County AL 0.714 0.270 0.444 2 Baldwin County AL 0.762 0.224 0.538 3 Barbour County AL 0.535 0.458 0.0766 R < 10 4 Bibb County AL 0.784 0.207 0.577 5 Blount County AL 0.896 0.0957 0.800 6 Bullock County AL 0.248 0.747 -0.499 7 Butler County AL 0.575 0.418 0.157 R + 10 8 Calhoun County AL 0.688 0.298 0.390 9 Chambers County AL 0.573 0.416 0.156 R + 10 10 Cherokee County AL 0.860 0.132 0.728 # ℹ 3,142 more rows ``` ::: ::: . . . And so on... ## 🚨 Your turn 🚨 Using the `elections` dataset: 1. Create a variable that tells you what happened in the 2016 election in each county. The variable should incorporate four possibilities: 1) DEMS won in 2012 and in 2016 ("blue") 2) REPS won in 2012 and in 2016 ("red") 3) the county flipped from blue to red ("blue to red") 4) the county flipped from red to blue ("red to blue") 2. Make a boxplot that compares household income (x-axis) across this variable (y-axis) ::: callout-tip 1. The elections data set is in the package `juanr` 2. Use `geom_boxplot()` for a boxplot graph ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## 🚨 Elections 🚨 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} elections_cat <- elections |> mutate( won = case_when( per_dem_2012 > per_gop_2012 & per_dem_2016 > per_gop_2016 ~ "blue", per_dem_2012 > per_gop_2012 & per_dem_2016 > per_gop_2016 ~ "red", per_dem_2012 < per_gop_2016 & per_dem_2016 > per_gop_2016 ~ "red to blue", per_dem_2012 > per_gop_2016 & per_dem_2016 < per_gop_2016 ~ "blue to red" ) ) # ggplot(data= elections_cat, aes(x = hh_income, y = won)) + geom_boxplot() ``` :::

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
